title: Features vs Scope Creep
intro: Scope creep kills projects. Judge whether each scenario is good planning discipline or a scope creep trap.

teach.title: The #1 Project Killer
teach.body: Most apps never ship. The reason isn't bad code or hard bugs — it's scope creep. That's when you keep adding 'just one more feature' until the project is so big you abandon it. Vibe coders are especially vulnerable because AI makes it feel easy to add features.

teach.title: The Restaurant Menu Problem
teach.body: A restaurant with 200 items on the menu can't be great at all of them. A focused menu with 10 items done well beats a bloated one every time. Your app is the same — 5 polished features beat 50 half-built ones.

teach.title: Packing for a Weekend Trip
teach.body: Scope creep is like packing a suitcase for a weekend trip but bringing clothes for two weeks 'just in case.' Suddenly your carry-on is overweight and you can't even get on the plane. Pack light, travel fast. You can always buy a toothbrush when you land — same way you can always add features after you ship.

teach.title: A Scope Creep Conversation
teach.code:
You: "I'll build a simple task manager"
Day 1: "Let me add categories too"
Day 3: "Tags would be nice"
Day 5: "What about recurring tasks?"
Day 7: "Maybe a calendar view?"
Day 14: "I should add notifications"
Day 30: *project abandoned*
teach.annotation: Each feature alone seems small, but they compound. Every feature means more code, more bugs, more edge cases.

teach.body: The apps making money on the app store aren't the ones with the most features — they're the ones that actually shipped. Every feature you cut gets you closer to launch day, and launch day is when you start earning.
Write down your MVP features BEFORE you start. If a new idea comes up, add it to a 'v2 ideas' list, not to your current sprint. Ship first, improve later.

cards.content: When you think of a cool feature, you write it in a 'Future Ideas' doc instead of building it right away
cards.explanation: This is the best way to handle feature ideas without losing them or derailing your project.

cards.content: You're almost done but think 'let me just add dark mode real quick before I ship'
cards.explanation: Classic scope creep. 'Real quick' features never are. Ship first, dark mode second.

cards.content: Before starting, you write down exactly 5 core features and commit to shipping only those
cards.explanation: A clear, limited feature list is your best defense against scope creep.

cards.content: A friend says 'you should add a Pomodoro timer' so you immediately start building it
cards.explanation: Feature requests from users are valuable — but they go on the v2 list, not the current sprint.

cards.content: You realize your app has 12 planned features and cut it down to 6 to ship faster
cards.explanation: Cutting scope is a sign of maturity. Shipping something small that works beats a big thing that doesn't.
